1: "Now you will see" — YHWH's response to Moses's lament begins with a promise of witness: Moses will observe what YHWH will do. "By a strong hand (beyad chazaqah) he will let them go; by a strong hand he will drive them out" — the double use of yad chazaqah (strong hand) frames the Exodus as an act of divine force that overwhelms even Pharaoh's coercive power. The phrase becomes the theological shorthand for the Exodus throughout the OT (Deuteronomy 6:21; Exodus 26:8).
2: "I am YHWH" — the covenant self-identification formula that anchors everything that follows. The declaration ani YHWH appears seven times in the seven "I will" passage of Exodus 6:2-8, functioning as the name-signature at both the opening and closing of the covenant formula. Every "I will" statement is grounded in the unshakeable identity of the speaker.
3: "I appeared to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob as El Shaddai, but by my name YHWH I was not known (noda'ti) to them" — the key interpretive problem: the patriarchs used the name YHWH (Genesis 12:8; Exodus 15:7), so in what sense was it "not known" to them? The key is the verb yada' (H3045): in Hebrew, to "know" involves not merely familiarity but experiential relationship — to know by encounter. The patriarchs heard the name; the Exodus generation will know its meaning through the redemptive events about to unfold. El Shaddai (H410 + H7706) — "God Almighty" (LXX/Vg: Pantokrator/Omnipotens); the etymology is disputed: shad (breast, a nurturing epithet?), shaddai from Akkadian shadu (mountain), or from shadad (to overwhelm). The patriarchal name encoded provision and power; the exodus name will encode redemption and covenant fidelity.
4: The covenant with the patriarchs is recalled: "I established my covenant (qumoti et-beriti, H6965 + H1285 — I caused my covenant to stand)" — the hiphil of qum emphasizes YHWH's active agency in maintaining the covenant. The land of Canaan as "the land of their sojournings (eretz megureihem)" — the patriarchs were gerim (sojourners) in the land that was promised but not yet possessed.
5: "I have also heard the groaning (ne'aqat, H5009) of the Israelites" — the same root as the "sighing and groaning" of Exodus 2:24. Na'aq is a cry of oppression, closer to a groan than a prayer; it is not yet liturgical intercession but the raw sound of suffering. "I have remembered (zakar, H2142) my covenant" — divine memory is not cognitive retrieval but covenantal activation; to "remember" a covenant is to act upon it.
6: The seven "I will" declarations begin: (1) "I will bring you out (hotzeti, H3318)" — the Exodus verb yatza (to go out) in its causative form; (2) "I will free you from being slaves (hitzalti, H5337)" — natsal (to snatch/rescue); (3) "I will redeem you (ga'alti, H1350) with an outstretched arm" — ga'al is the kinsman-redeemer verb, the obligation of the nearest male relative to buy back enslaved kin (Leviticus 25:47-49; Ruth 3:13). YHWH applies to himself the legal role of the go'el — he has kinship obligations to Israel and will fulfill them even at cost. This is one of the most legally precise theological statements in the OT.
7: (4) "I will take you as my own people (laqachti etkem li le'am)" — the covenant-adoption formula; (5) "I will be your God (vehayiti lakhem le-Elohim)" — the bilateral covenant formula (cf. "you will be my people and I will be your God," Jeremiah 30:22; Ezekiel 36:28). Then the epistemological purpose: "you will know (yeda'tem) that I am YHWH your God who brought you out from under the yokes of Egypt." The yada' that was denied to the patriarchs will now be granted to the Exodus generation through the redemptive events themselves.
8: (6) "I will bring you to the land" and (7) "I will give it to you as a possession (morashah, H4181, inheritance/possession)." Morashah appears only here and in Deuteronomy 33:4 ("the Torah is Moses's inheritance for the congregation of Jacob") in the Pentateuch; it is the legal term for heritable property. "I am YHWH" — the formula closes the seven-fold declaration, bracketing it with the divine name as its unshakeable foundation.
9: "They did not listen to Moses because of their discouragement (qotser ruach, H7114 + H7307 — shortness/exhaustion of spirit) and harsh labor (avodah qashah)." Qotser ruach is literally "shortness of breath" — the oppressed person who cannot breathe freely. The trauma of slavery impairs reception of hope: even the most liberating message cannot be received by a spirit that has been systematically crushed. This verse is a pastoral-theological observation: suffering can temporarily block the capacity for faith, without that block being a moral failure.
10: YHWH's instruction to Moses and the simple narrative marker "the LORD spoke to Moses, saying" — a formulaic introduction used repeatedly throughout Exodus and Leviticus. Its repetition creates the rhythm of continuous divine speech that structures the Pentateuch.
11: The commission is restated: "Go, tell Pharaoh king of Egypt to let the Israelites go out of his country." The simplicity of the command contrasts with the elaborate commissioning of Exodus 3-4; after Moses's lament and YHWH's response, the mission is re-launched in its most elemental form.
12: Moses raises the objection again: "if the Israelites will not listen to me, why would Pharaoh listen to me, since I speak with uncircumcised lips (aral sefatayim)?" Arel (H6189, uncircumcised) when applied to non-physical features in Hebrew means "blocked, stopped up, ritually unready" (cf. uncircumcised heart, Deuteronomy 10:16; uncircumcised ear, Jeremiah 6:10). Moses's "uncircumcised lips" are lips that are blocked from effective speech — not in need of literal circumcision but of covenant opening. The repetition of this objection (first in Exodus 4:10) frames it as a persistent self-perception that YHWH will eventually correct not through argument but through experience.
13: YHWH's response to Moses's renewed objection is to commission both Moses and Aaron together. The "charging" (vaytsav, H6680) of them both — a term used for formal official orders — authorizes the joint mission with full divine authority.
14: The genealogical register begins: Reuben's sons — Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi. This interruption of the narrative just before the plagues serves to anchor Moses and Aaron in their full tribal lineage. The genealogy moves quickly through Reuben and Simeon to reach Levi, the tribe that matters for the commission context.
15: Simeon's sons listed, including "Shaul the son of a Canaanite woman" — the ethnically mixed ancestry is noted without comment, consistent with the text's honest inclusion of narratives that complicate the purity of covenant lineage (cf. Judah and Tamar, Gen 38).
16: Levi's three sons — Gershon, Kohath, and Merari — and his total lifespan of 137 years. The Levite genealogy is the point of the entire insert; Reuben and Simeon are listed to establish that the genealogy is ordered by birth sequence before arriving at Levi. Kohath (Moses's grandfather's line) gets the most detail.
17: Gershon's sons: Libni and Shimei — the priestly clans that will serve in the Kohathite-Gershonite-Merarite division of Levitical service (Num 3-4).
18: Kohath's sons: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, Uzziel. Kohath lived 133 years. Amram (H6019, "the people are exalted") — Moses and Aaron's father; his name connects the family to the collective identity of the people being liberated.
19: Merari's sons: Mahli and Mushi. The three clans of Levi are now established — Gershon, Kohath, Merari — each with their own sub-clans that will be assigned distinct roles in the tabernacle service.
20: Amram married Jochebed (Yocheved, H3115, "YHWH is her glory") his father's sister — a marriage that Leviticus 18:12 will later prohibit ("do not have sexual relations with your father's sister"). The genealogy records the union without apology: the Sinai legislation had not yet been given; the patriarchal and early Mosaic periods precede the codification of these prohibitions. Jochebed is described as bearing "Aaron and Moses" — Aaron is named first, consistent with his role as elder brother (Exodus 7:7).
21: Izhar's sons: Korah, Nepheg, Zichri. Korah (H7141) — the rebel whose challenge to Moses and Aaron will be narrated in Num 16-17; the genealogy introduces him without comment here, establishing him as Moses's first cousin before his rebellion is recorded.
22: Uzziel's sons: Mishael, Elzaphan, and Sithri. Elzaphan (Elizaphan) appears again in Numbers 3:30 as the leader of the Kohathite clan; his genealogical prominence here anticipates his later administrative role.
23: Aaron married Elisheba, daughter of Amminadab and sister of Nahshon. Nahshon (H5177) was the prince of Judah (Numbers 1:7; Exodus 2:3) and a direct ancestor of David (Ruth 4:20) and of Jesus (Matthew 1:4; Luke 3:32). Aaron's marriage connects the Levitical priesthood to the Davidic-Judahite line through the marriage of the first high priest.
24: The sons of Korah are listed — Assir, Elkanah, Abiasaph. These become the "Korahite" Levites; Num 16 records Korah's rebellion, but the Korahite clan persists in priestly service (1 Chronicles 9:19; Pss 42, 44-49, 84-85, 87-88 are "Psalms of the Sons of Korah"). The genealogical record includes without erasing even the lineage that produces rebellion.
25: Eleazar son of Aaron married a daughter of Putiel, and she bore him Phinehas. Phinehas (H6372) will become the paradigm of covenant zeal (Numbers 25:7-11 — he acts decisively in the Baal-Peor incident and receives "the covenant of peace" and a "covenant of everlasting priesthood"). The genealogy is establishing the priestly lineage that will run through Eleazar and Phinehas, not through Nadab and Abihu who will die offering unauthorized fire (Leviticus 10:1-2).
26: "It was this same Aaron and Moses (hu Aaron u-Moshe) to whom the LORD said..." — the resumptive identification closes the genealogical bracket, re-anchoring the narrative to the two named protagonists. The genealogy has established their credentials; now the text can proceed. The reversal of order ("Aaron and Moses" instead of "Moses and Aaron") may signal Aaron's prominence as spokesman in the sequence that follows.
27: The same Moses and Aaron who appeared before Pharaoh are those who brought Israel out of Egypt — the genealogy has anchored them in the covenant lineage; now the narrative resumes with their commission confirmed and their identity established before the plague sequence begins.
28: The temporal marker "on the day the LORD spoke to Moses in Egypt" — a resumptive temporal clause that re-anchors the narrative timeline after the genealogical digression. The formal date-formula is used in Leviticus and Numbers to introduce divine-speech units (Leviticus 1:1, "called to Moses and spoke to him from the tent of meeting, saying").
29: "I am YHWH — tell Pharaoh king of Egypt everything I tell you." The commission is restated in its simplest form: the entire content of the message is "everything I tell you" — total obedience to the divine word, not selective transmission. The formula ani YHWH again: the commission is grounded in identity, not argument.
30: "Since I speak with faltering lips, why would Pharaoh listen to me?" — Moses repeats the "uncircumcised lips" objection from Exodus 6:12. The repetition (Exodus 6:12, Exodus 6:30) creates a bracket around the genealogy: the same objection before and after the family credentials are established. Despite his genealogical legitimacy, Moses still distrusts his own speech. YHWH's response will not be to fix Moses's speech but to work through him anyway — the ultimate answer to the "faltering lips" objection is the plagues themselves.
